 Reserved


Error Message

Error messages report class-C error events to UBFM, including Correctable Error, Non-Fatal
Uncorrectable Error, and Fatal Uncorrectable Error events, all following the same PDU format.

            Byte 0                      Byte 1                    Byte 2                     Byte 3
  7     6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0
            RSVD                                                  SCNA
 EL                         RSVD                                              Port_idx
                               Figure 10-35 PDU format of error messages

The PDUs in error messages follow the format illustrated above, and their fields are defined as follows:

      1.   SCNA: contains 24 bits and indicates the network address of the node where the error
           occurs.
      2.   Port_idx: contains 16 bits and indicates the index of the port in error. This field is valid when
           the value of EL is 0.
      3.   EL: contains 1 bit and indicates the error level. The value 0 indicates a port-level error, while
           the value 1 indicates a device-level error.

Link Change Message

      1.   Link Up/Down Message
           Link change messages report status change events of port links, including Link Up and Link
           Down events, to trigger dynamic device connection or disconnection, link failover, and link
           failback by UBFM. Both events follow the same PDU format.
           Byte 0                     Byte 1                     Byte 2                     Byte 3
 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0
           RSVD                                                  SCNA
                       RSVD                                                  Port_idx
                            Figure 10-36 PDU format of link change messages




unifiedbus.com                                                                                           336
10 Resource Management



            The PDUs in link change messages follow the format illustrated above, and their fields are
            defined as follows:
            (1) SCNA: contains 24 bits and indicates the network address of the port whose link status
                    changes. If no network address has been configured for the port, the network address of
                    the UB Controller or UB Switch is reported.
            (2) Port_idx: contains 16 bits and indicates the index of the port whose link status changes.
      2.    Hot-plug Message
            Hot-plug messages report events related to the working status of slots, including Hot-plug
            Button Pressed and Hot-plug Presence Detect Changed, both following the same PDU
            format.
            Byte 0                        Byte 1                     Byte 2                     Byte 3
 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0
                           RSVD                                               Slot_idx
                                                       RSVD
                                    Figure 10-37 PDU format of hot-plug messages

            The PDUs in hot-plug messages follow the format illustrated above, and their field is defined
            as follows:
            Slot_idx: contains 16 bits and indicates the index of the slot that reports the event.

Configuration Message

Configuration messages are used to read and write configuration space registers. Each message
carries only one operation, either a request or a response, as indicated by the MGMTETAH.Rsp field.
Requests and responses follow different PDU formats.

           Byte 0                      Byte 1                  Byte 2                        Byte 3
7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0                               7     6    5     4      3 2 1 0
                       Entity_idx                              RSVD                Byte_En             RSVD
                                                   Request Address
                                                   RSVD (4 bytes)
                                                        Data
                           Figure 10-38 PDU format of configuration request messages

The PDUs in configuration request messages follow the format illustrated above, and their fields are
defined as follows:

      1.    Byte_En: indicates whether to enable byte write. It contains 4 bits, corresponding to the
            enable bits of the 4-byte data.
            −   1: enable
            −   0: disable




unifiedbus.com                                                                                                337
10 Resource Management



      2.    Entity_idx: contains 16 bits and indicates the Entity index.
      3.    Request Address: contains 4 bytes and indicates the request address, in the unit of 4 bytes.
      4.    Data: contains 4 bytes and indicates the configuration data.
           Byte 0                     Byte 1                     Byte 2                   Byte 3
 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0
                                                    Data
                                               RSVD (4 bytes)
                                               RSVD (4 bytes)
                                               RSVD (4 bytes)
                       Figure 10-39 PDU format of configuration response messages

The PDUs in configuration response messages follow the format illustrated above, and their field is
defined as follows:

Data: contains 4 bytes and indicates the data returned in the configuration read response.

Vendor-Defined Message (VDM)

      1.    MCTP Over UB
            MCTP messages (DMTF DSP0236) are transmitted over UB links as PDUs of configuration
            management messages, without any modification.
      2.    VDM for UB Spec
            VDM for UB Spec is reserved by this specification. Its PDU format is as follows:
           Byte 0                     Byte 1                     Byte 2                   Byte 3
 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0
                                            UB Spec. Definition
                               Figure 10-40 PDU format of VDM for UB Spec

      3.    VDM for Vendor
            VDM for Vendor is defined by vendors. Its PDU format is as follows:
           Byte 0                     Byte 1                     Byte 2                   Byte 3
 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0
  Version           Type                                          RSVD
                       Vendor ID                                           Device ID
                                               Vendor Definition
                                Figure 10-41 PDU format of VDM for Vendor

The fields corresponding to the first eight bytes are from the GUID and are used for verification.




unifiedbus.com                                                                                        338
10 Resource Management



Entity Information Exchange Message

     1.    Resource Space Information Exchange
           Resource Space Information Exchange messages are used to accelerate reads from and
           writes to resource space configuration register groups. The length of PDUs in Resource
           Space Information Exchange messages depends on the value of Opcode in requests: 0 for 4
           bytes and 1 for 28 bytes in request messages; 0 for 36 bytes and 1 for 0 bytes in response
           messages.
          Byte 0                     Byte 1                      Byte 2               Byte 3
 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1                                      0




                                                                                                    Opcode
                                                RSVD


                                                ERS0_SA0
                                                ERS0_SA1
                                                ERS1_SA0
                                                ERS1_SA1
                                                ERS2_SA0
                                                ERS2_SA1
          Figure 10-42 PDU format of Resource Space Information Exchange request messages

           The PDUs in Resource Space Information Exchange request messages follow the format
           illustrated above, and their fields are defined as follows:
           (1) Opcode: indicates the operation code.
                 −   0: Obtain the resource space information.
                 −   1: Configure the resource space information.
           (2) ERS0_SA: contains 8 bytes and indicates the start address of resource space 0.
           (3) ERS1_SA: contains 8 bytes and indicates the start address of resource space 1.
           (4) ERS2_SA: contains 8 bytes and indicates the start address of resource space 2.
          Byte 0                     Byte 1                       Byte 2               Byte 3
 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0
                                                 ERS0_SS
                                                ERS0_SA0
                                                ERS0_SA1
                                                 ERS1_SS
                                                ERS1_SA0
                                                ERS1_SA1




unifiedbus.com                                                                                      339
10 Resource Management



          Byte 0                      Byte 1                     Byte 2                        Byte 3
 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0
                                                  ERS2_SS
                                                 ERS2_SA0
                                                 ERS2_SA1
          Figure 10-43 PDU format of Resource Space Information Exchange response messages

           The PDUs in Resource Space Information Exchange response messages follow the format
           illustrated above, and their fields are defined as follows:
           (1) ERS0_SS: contains 4 bytes and indicates the size of resource space 0, in the unit
                 specified by SYS_PGS. The field value is 0 when there is no resource space 0.
           (2) ERS1_SS: contains 4 bytes and indicates the size of resource space 1, in the unit
                 specified by SYS_PGS. The field value is 0 when there is no resource space 1.
           (3) ERS2_SS: contains 4 bytes and indicates the size of resource space 2, in the unit
                 specified by SYS_PGS. The field value is 0 when there is no resource space 2.
           (4) ERS0_SA: contains 8 bytes and indicates the start address of resource space 0.
           (5) ERS1_SA: contains 8 bytes and indicates the start address of resource space 1.
           (6) ERS2_SA: contains 8 bytes and indicates the start address of resource space 2.
     2.    Obtaining Entity Information
          Byte 0                     Byte 1                     Byte 2                      Byte 3
 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1                                          0
                                                    RSVD
                      MUE nums                                              Entity nums
                  MUE0's end UE idx                                      MUE0's start UE idx
                  MUE1's end UE idx                                      MUE1's start UE idx
                                                      ...
                  MUEx's end UE idx                                      MUEx's start UE idx
                 Figure 10-44 PDU format of Obtaining Entity Information response messages

           Obtaining Entity Information request messages do not carry PDUs, but response messages
           do. The PDUs in response messages follow the format illustrated above, and their fields are
           defined as follows:
           (1) Entity nums: contains 2 bytes and indicates the total number of Entities.
           (2) MUE nums: contains 2 bytes and indicates the number of MUEs.
           (3) MUE[i]'s start UE idx: contains 2 bytes and indicates the start index of UEs owned by
                 the ith MUE. If the value of this field is equal to i, no UE is bound to the MUE.
           (4) MUE[i]'s end UE idx: contains 2 bytes and indicates the end index of UEs owned by the
                 ith MUE. If the value of this field is equal to i, no UE is bound to the MUE.



unifiedbus.com                                                                                          340
10 Resource Management



      3.   Link Neighbor Query
           UBFM uses the Link Neighbor Query command to query the neighbors of a specified port set
           on a destination node. A PDU in a Link Neighbor Query response message carries one or
           more Port_info TLVs of topology query responses, enabling functions such as incremental
           enumeration.
           Byte 0                     Byte 1                    Byte 2                    Byte 3
 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0
 D          RSVD                    Port_num                                 RSVD
                        Port_idx0                                           Port_idx1
                        Port_idx2                                              ...
                     Figure 10-45 PDU format of Link Neighbor Query request messages

The PDUs in Link Neighbor Query request messages follow the format illustrated above, and their fields
are defined as follows:

           (1) D (discrete): contains 1 bit and indicates the query mode.
                 −   0: batch query of consecutive port indexes, which start from Port_idx0 and end with
                     Port_idx1
                 −   1: batch query of discrete port indexes—Port_idx0, Port_idx1, Port_idx2, and so on
           (2) Port_num: contains 1 byte and indicates the number of ports to be queried. UBFM
                 SHALL ensure that the size of the response message does not exceed the capacity of a
                 management message.
           (3) Port_idx: contains 2 bytes and indicates the port index to be queried.
           Byte 0                     Byte 1                    Byte 2                   Byte 3
 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0
                                               Port_info TLVs
                     Figure 10-46 PDU format of Link Neighbor Query response messages

           The PDUs in Link Neighbor Query response messages follow the format illustrated above,
           and their field is defined as follows:
           Port_info TLVs: indicates one or more TLVs returned in the response message, with the
           format defined in "Topology Query" in Section 10.4.3.2.

Device Security Management Message

      1.   Device Authentication Interaction
           The format of PDUs in Device Authentication Interaction messages follows the SPDM
           (DMTF DSP0274).
      2.   Token Check Configuration
           Token Check Configuration enables or disables devices to check tokens for resource space
           access requests. By default, token check is disabled on devices. Configuration request



unifiedbus.com                                                                                        341
10 Resource Management



              messages always carry 4-byte PDUs. The response messages for enabling token check
              carry 8-byte PDUs, while those for disabling token check do not carry PDUs.
             Byte 0                    Byte 1                   Byte 2                    Byte 3
 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1                                           0




                                                                                                         Check_En
                                                   RSVD


                   Figure 10-47 PDU format of Token Check Configuration request messages

              The PDUs in Token Check Configuration request messages follow the format illustrated
              above, and their field is defined as follows:
              Check_En: contains 1 bit and indicates whether to enable token check. The value 0 means
              to disable, while the value 1 means to enable.
             Byte 0                     Byte 1                   Byte 2                     Byte 3
 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0
                      RSVD                                               TokenID
                                                   TokenValue
                  Figure 10-48 PDU format of Token Check Configuration response messages

              The PDUs in Token Check Configuration response messages follow the format illustrated
              above, and their fields are defined as follows:
              (1) TokenID: contains 20 bits and indicates the token ID that SHALL be carried for
                  accessing the resource space.
              (2) TokenValue: contains 32 bits and indicates the token value carried for accessing the
                  resource space.

Pooled Resource Management Message

        1.    Entity Registration
              UBFM uses the Entity Registration command to allocate pooled Entities to users. Entity
              Registration request messages always carry 92-byte PDUs, while response messages do
              not carry PDUs.
             Byte 0                      Byte 1                  Byte 2                     Byte 3
 7      6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0
 RSVD




                              UPI                                            Entity_idx


                                                       EID0
                                                       EID1
                                                       EID2




unifiedbus.com                                                                                           342
10 Resource Management



         Byte 0                      Byte 1                    Byte 2                    Byte 3
 7   6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0
                                                   EID3
                                                  GUID0
                                                  GUID1
                                                  GUID2
                                                  GUID3
         RSVD                                                   CNA
                                                  UEID0
                                                  UEID1
                                                  UEID2
                                                  UEID3
                                                 ERS0_SS
                                                 ERS0_SA0
                                                 ERS0_SA1
                                                 ERS1_SS
                                                 ERS1_SA0
                                                 ERS1_SA1
                                                 ERS2_SS
                                                 ERS2_SA0
                                                 ERS2_SA1
                     Figure 10-49 PDU format of Entity Registration request messages

          The PDUs in Entity Registration request messages follow the format illustrated above, and
          their fields are defined as follows:
          (1) Entity_idx: contains 16 bits and indicates the index of the Entity to be registered for use
                 by the user.
          (2) UPI: contains 15 bits and indicates the UPI of the Entity to be registered for use by
                 the user.
          (3) EID: contains 128 bits and indicates the EID of the Entity to be registered for use by
                 the user.
          (4) GUID: contains 128 bits and indicates the GUID of the Entity to be registered for use by
                 the user.
          (5) CNA: contains 24 bits and indicates the CNA of the Entity to be registered for use by
                 the user.
          (6) UEID: contains 128 bits and indicates the EID of the user.
          (7) ERS0_SS: contains 32 bits and indicates the address space size of resource space 0, in
                 the unit specified by SYS_PGS. The field value is 0 when there is no resource space 0.
          (8) ERS0_SA: contains 64 bits and indicates the start address of resource space 0.



unifiedbus.com                                                                                        343
10 Resource Management



          (9) ERS1_SS: contains 32 bits and indicates the address space size of resource space 1, in
                 the unit specified by SYS_PGS. The field value is 0 when there is no resource space 1.
          (10) ERS1_SA: contains 64 bits and indicates the start address of resource space 1.
          (11) ERS2_SS: contains 32 bits and indicates the address space size of resource space 2, in
                 the unit specified by SYS_PGS. The field value is 0 when there is no resource space 2.
          (12) ERS2_SA: contains 64 bits and indicates the start address of resource space 2.
     2.   Entity Deregistration
          UBFM uses the Entity Deregistration command to reclaim allocated Entities from users.
          Entity Deregistration request messages always carry 20-byte PDUs, while response
          messages do not carry PDUs.
          Byte 0                      Byte 1                   Byte 2                    Byte 3
 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0
                                                   EID0
                                                   EID1
                                                   EID2
                                                   EID3
                                      RSVD                                               Reason
                     Figure 10-50 PDU format of Entity Deregistration request messages

          The PDUs in Entity Deregistration request messages follow the format illustrated above, and
          their fields are defined as follows:
          (1) EID: contains 128 bits and indicates the EID of the allocated Entity.
          (2) Reason: contains 8 bits and indicates the reason for deregistration.
                 −   0x00: normal deregistration
                 −   0x01: device error
                 −   0x02: abnormal device link
                 −   0x03 to 0xFF: reserved
     3.   Bus Instance Create
          The Bus Instance Create command is used to create entries for VMs to access the bus.
          Its request messages always carry 36-byte PDUs, while its response messages do not
          carry PDUs.
          Byte 0                      Byte 1                   Byte 2                    Byte 3
 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0
                                                   GUID0
                                                   GUID1
                                                   GUID2
                                                   GUID3




unifiedbus.com                                                                                      344
10 Resource Management



          Byte 0                         Byte 1                      Byte 2                        Byte 3
 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0
                                                       EID0
                                                       EID1
                                                       EID2
                                                       EID3
                           RSVD                                                        UPI
                    Figure 10-51 PDU format of Bus Instance Create request messages

          The PDUs in Bus Instance Create request messages follow the format illustrated above, and
          their fields are defined as follows:
          (1) GUID: contains 128 bits and indicates the GUID of the Bus Instance.
          (2) EID: contains 128 bits and indicates the EID of the Bus Instance.
          (3) UPI: contains 15 bits and indicates the UPI of the Bus Instance.
          Note: If a UBPU has multiple bus controllers, the bus controller instance created by running this command
          takes effect on all these bus controllers.

     4.   Bus Instance Destroy
          The Bus Instance Destroy command is used to destroy the entries for VMs to access the
          bus. Its request messages always carry 16-byte PDUs, while its response messages do not
          carry PDUs.
          Byte 0                         Byte 1                      Byte 2                        Byte 3
 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0
                                                       GUID0
                                                       GUID1
                                                       GUID2
                                                       GUID3
                   Figure 10-52 PDU format of Bus Instance Destroy request messages

          The PDUs in Bus Instance Destroy request messages follow the format illustrated above,
          and their field is defined as follows:
          GUID: contains 128 bits and indicates the GUID of the Bus Instance.
     5.   Configuration Completion Notification
          The Configuration Completion Notification command is used to notify UBPUs that the bus
          controller configuration is complete and the UB Bus Driver is ready to provide services for
          the upper layer. Its request messages carry 36-byte PDUs, while its response messages do
          not carry PDUs.




unifiedbus.com                                                                                                   345
10 Resource Management



          Byte 0                       Byte 1                         Byte 2                         Byte 3
 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0
                                                     RSVD                                                             F
                                                         GUID0
                                                         GUID1
                                                         GUID2
                                                         GUID3
                                                         EID0
                                                         EID1
                                                         EID2
                                                         EID3
           Figure 10-53 PDU format of Configuration Completion Notification request messages

          The PDUs in Configuration Completion Notification request messages follow the format
          illustrated above, and their fields are defined as follows:
          (1) GUID: contains 128 bits and indicates the GUID of the bus controller instance. This field
                 is valid when the value of F is 1.
          (2) EID: contains 128 bits and indicates the EID of the bus controller instance. This field is
                 valid when the value of F is 1.
          (3) F: indicates whether to create a bus controller instance for the UBPU. The value 0
                 means not, while the value 1 means to create.
          Note: If a UBPU has multiple bus controllers, the bus controller instance created by running this command
          takes effect on all these bus controllers. When the bus controller instance is created, it SHALL be assigned
          the same UPI as that of the bus controllers.

     6.   Port Reset Notification
          UBFM uses the Port Reset Notification command to instruct UBPUs to perform reset
          operations on the ports of bus controllers, including preparing for and completing port reset.
          After receiving a reset preparation notification, a UBPU SHALL make preparations for the
          reset, including stopping traffic on other controllers that share the port with the bus controller.
          Port Reset Notification request messages always carry 4-byte PDUs, while response
          messages do not carry PDUs.
          Byte 0                       Byte 1                         Byte 2                        Byte 3
    7 65 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0
                       Port_idx                                                    RSVD                               T
                    Figure 10-54 PDU format of Port Reset Notification request messages

          The PDUs in Port Reset Notification request messages follow the format illustrated above,
          and their fields are defined as follows:




unifiedbus.com                                                                                                     346
10 Resource Management



           (1) T: contains 1 bit and indicates the type of the port reset notification. The value 0
                 indicates port reset preparation, while the value 1 indicates port reset completion.
           (2) Port_idx: contains 16 bits and indicates the index of the port to be reset.


10.4.4 Link Neighbor Advertisement
Link neighbor advertisement enables two UBPUs connected by a UB link to exchange neighbor
information via link-layer Param_Exchg blocks. The neighbor information includes:

      1.   Value in the Port Index register
      2.   GUID of Entity 0

System administrators can enable or disable the link neighbor advertisement function based on the
enumeration range. After receiving a link neighbor advertisement, a UBPU stores the port index and
GUID carried in the advertisement to the Neighbor Port Info register group. The information in the
Neighbor Port Info register group is valid only when port links are up. If a port link is down, the UBPU
SHALL clear the information in the Neighbor Port Info register group to prevent software from reading
incorrect information during dynamic connection or disconnection of neighboring nodes.

A Param_Exchg block used for a link neighbor advertisement includes five flits (flit 0 to flit 4), whose
formats are as follows:

Flit0.PARAMETER_EXCHANGE_DATA.Byte0-Byte3={RSVD[15:0],port_idx[15:0]}

Flit1.PARAMETER_EXCHANGE_DATA.Byte0-Byte3={GUID[31:0]}

Flit2.PARAMETER_EXCHANGE_DATA.Byte0-Byte3={GUID[63:32]}

Flit3.PARAMETER_EXCHANGE_DATA.Byte0-Byte3={GUID[95:64]}

Flit4.PARAMETER_EXCHANGE_DATA.Byte0-Byte3={GUID[127:96]}


10.4.5 Pooled Entity Management

10.4.5.1 Management Role

Functions or services specified by all Entities in a UB domain form a shareable resource pool. The
pooled Entity resources can be managed by one role and used by another: UBPUs provide and use
these resources, while UBFM manages these resources and dynamically allocates them to the UBPUs.
Entity 0 is responsible for the common configuration management of UB Controllers and SHALL NOT
be allocated to untrusted UBPUs.




unifiedbus.com                                                                                             347
10 Resource Management




                                 Figure 10-55 Pooled Entity management


10.4.5.2 Registration Process

UBFM discovers and manages pooled resources, including configuring network addresses and routes.
The registration process of pooled resources is as follows:

      1.   UBFM configures an EID, UPI, and UEID for an Entity.
      2.   UBFM sends an Entity registration command to allocate the Entity configured in step 1 to
           the user.
      3.   Upon receiving the registration command, the UB Bus Driver of the user registers the Entity
           with the UB bus in the OS and returns a response to UBFM.
      4.   After the registration is successful, the user creates a device in the OS, loads the device
           driver, and starts to use the device functions.




                                    Figure 10-56 Registration process

In the preceding process, UBFM SHALL:

After sending an Entity registration command to the user, wait for a response to confirm that the
registration is complete. If no response is received within a period of time (determined by the UBFM
software), the registration fails and UBFM reclaims the Entity.



unifiedbus.com                                                                                           348
10 Resource Management



In the preceding process, the user SHALL:

      1.   Read the device information of the specified Entity to confirm that the Entity is available.
      2.   After receiving a registration command from UBFM, record the registration behavior and
           return only one response.
      3.   Be able to call remote device functions based on the local UB Controller, which can be a bus
           controller, network controller, or the like.


10.4.5.3 Deregistration Process

The deregistration process is as follows:

      1.   UBFM sends an Entity deregistration command to instruct the user to deregister the
           allocated Entity.
      2.   The user deregisters the device resources, stops accessing the remote device, and destroys
           the device created in the OS.
      3.   After the deregistration is complete, the user returns a response to UBFM.
      4.   UBFM resets the deregistered Entity and reclaims it.




                                    Figure 10-57 Deregistration process

In the preceding process, UBFM SHALL:

After sending an Entity deregistration command to the user, wait for a response to confirm that the
deregistration is complete and then reclaim the Entity. If no response is received within a period of time
(determined by the UBFM software), the deregistration fails and UBFM forcibly reclaims the Entity.

In the preceding process, the user SHALL:

      1.   After receiving a deregistration command from UBFM, record the deregistration behavior and
           return only one response.
      2.   When deregistering the Entity, start by deregistering the resources allocated to the Entity in
           the OS to prevent the user itself or any other UBPU authorized by the user from accessing
           the Entity again.




unifiedbus.com                                                                                            349
10 Resource Management



10.4.5.4 Replacement Process

If an uncorrectable error occurs on an Entity allocated to a user, UBFM can select another Entity,
migrate the basic configuration of the faulty Entity to the selected Entity, and register the selected Entity
with the user as a replacement. The implementation of remote device replacement on the user side is
restricted by the device functions specified by Entities and is not defined in this specification.


10.4.6 Communication Control




         Figure 10-58 Reference process of communication control in standard transmission mode

System administrators MAY enable communication control between UBPUs when needed. The
preceding figure shows the process of communication control between UBPUs a and b in the standard
transmission mode: (1) UBPUs a and b apply to UBFM for communication; (2) UBFM determines
whether UBPUs a and b can communicate with each other based on the policy preset by a system
administrator, and if the policy allows, authorizes UBPUs a and b to communicate with each other; (3)
UBPUs a and b negotiate TP channels; (4) UBPUs a and b report the negotiation result to UBFM; (5)
UBFM activates the TP channels for UBPUs a and b; and (6) UBPUs a and b start to communicate with
each other.

In CTP or TP bypass mode, UBFM can control the communication between UBPUs by controlling the
entry configuration of UB Controller routing tables, that is, delivering routing table entries only when
communication is allowed.




unifiedbus.com                                                                                             350
10 Resource Management



10.4.7 Remote Memory Registration Control




                 Figure 10-59 Reference process of remote memory registration control

System administrators MAY enable centralized control of remote memory registration on UBPUs when
needed. When centralized control of remote memory registration is enabled, the Decoder MATT
jurisdiction register of UB Controllers SHALL be set to the UBFM control mode. The preceding figure
shows the process of remote memory registration control on UBPU a: (1) UBFM queries the resource
space address of an Entity or obtains the memory segment address of another UBPU other than UBPU
a; (2) UBFM writes the address to the Decoder MATT of UBPU a; and (3) the processor core of UBPU a
can access the remote memory by using load/store.




unifiedbus.com                                                                                        351
10 Resource Management



10.5 Virtualization

10.5.1 Virtualization Mechanism




                               Figure 10-60 UB-based virtualization system

UB MAY support hardware-assisted device virtualization, allowing Entities to be passed through to VMs
using the hypervisor virtualization software stack and system platform hardware. In the virtualization
system illustrated above, the hosts contain software and hardware components, including processors,
memory, UMMUs, hypervisors, and UB Controllers (which can be bus controllers or the like). UBFM can
allocate pooled Entities to a specified VM. The allocated Entities can be uninstalled from the VM, and
then reallocated to another VM after security processing (for example, Entity-level reset).

When starting a VM, the hypervisor virtualization software stack SHALL first create an independent
Entity as an entry for the VM to access the bus. This Entity is assigned an EID and a UPI. By using
Entity EIDs, devices can access VMs' memory and VMs can access each other's memory. VMs and
devices of different users are isolated by UB partitions and cannot access each other. For details, see
Section 10.3.




unifiedbus.com                                                                                           352
10 Resource Management




                                    Figure 10-61 Virtualization mechanism

After a host OS allocates resources to an Entity, the hypervisor SHALL implement device virtualization by:

      1.   Packaging the Entity as a device for the VM to use, with the data plane passed through to
           the VM and the management plane taken over by the hypervisor.
           (1) The data plane of the Entity handles resource space accesses, interrupts, and device
                 function data flows. The host of the VM cannot access this data plane.
           (2) The hypervisor manages the Entity by accessing its configuration spaces.
      2.   Supporting the UB bus, simulating an interconnection structure based on the topology
           specified by the administrator, and presenting the structure to the VM.


10.5.2 Virtualization Management

10.5.2.1 Entity Initialization

Before being used, an Entity SHALL be initialized by performing the following operations:

      1.   Configure an EID and a UPI.
      2.   Configure resource spaces.


10.5.2.2 Entity Interrupt

In virtualization scenarios, Entity interrupts can be passed through to VMs.


10.5.2.3 Entity Reset

In virtualization scenarios, Entity-level reset can be performed: (1) on a device to be replaced to ensure
information security and restore the Entity to the initial state; and (2) before an Entity is imported into a
VM to restore the Entity to the initial state.




unifiedbus.com                                                                                             353
10 Resource Management



10.6 RAS

10.6.1 Reset

10.6.1.1 General Requirements

A UBPU SHALL support the reset function, including device-level, Entity-level, and port-level reset.

A UBPU SHALL support reset upon device management command delivery and MAY support reset
triggered by pin signals, as well as self-reset. Self-reset is not defined in this specification.


10.6.1.2 Device-Level Reset

10.6.1.2.1 Scope

Device-level reset restores all Entities, their specified functions or services, and ports in a UBPU to their
initial state at power-on. During device-level reset, unfinished tasks may encounter exceptions or
response timeouts.


10.6.1.2.2 Management-Command-Triggered Reset

The device-level reset register only exists in Entity 0.

Upon receiving a device-level reset command, a UBPU executes the reset operation without
responding. A UBFM MAY query the reset progress after a delay.


10.6.1.2.3 Pin-Signal-Triggered Reset

A UBPU MAY support reset triggered by a signal line. When the level of the line meets the
requirements, the reset operation is triggered.


10.6.1.3 Entity-Level Reset

Entity-level reset restores an Entity's configuration space 1 and resource spaces to their initial state at
power-on. It does not affect public resource configurations at the network layer, data link layer, and
physical layer.

Users MAY use configuration management commands to reset their respective Entities.

Note: It is RECOMMENDED that an Entity reset be completed as soon as the reset command is received. Ensure that
the Entity can be normally accessed after the reset.


10.6.1.4 Port-Level Reset

Port-level reset restores the configuration of a specified port to its initial state at power-on. After the
reset, the port SHALL be reconfigured. A UBFM uses configuration management commands to write the
reset register of a port, thus achieving port-level reset.



unifiedbus.com                                                                                                354
10 Resource Management



When a port is in the reset state, it SHALL be described as unavailable in the routing table. If all ports of
a UBPU are in the reset state, the UBPU is inaccessible.


10.6.2 Error Handling

10.6.2.1 General Requirements

UB defines a series of error detection, handling, recording, and reporting mechanisms to support fault
locating and recovery. Error handling SHALL comply with the following principles:

      1.   Prevent errors from spreading to minimize their impact.
      2.   Correct errors within the shortest time possible.
      3.   Handle errors locally by type.
      4.   Record errors.


10.6.2.2 Error Types

10.6.2.2.1 Error Classification

This specification defines three classes of error events: A, B, and C.

      1.   In the case of a class-A error, the physical topology of the UB Fabric remains intact. The
           error can be traced to a specific transaction and does not affect the execution of other
           transactions. If an error occurs during the execution of a single SQE in an SQ, it can typically
           be reported through a CQ to software for handling.
      2.   In the case of a class-B error, the physical topology of the UB Fabric remains intact. The
           error can be traced to a specific Entity, TP channel, or Jetty (Entity-level, TP channel-level,
           or Jetty-level error) rather than a specific transaction. All resources of the unit in error need
           to be processed. Such errors can typically be reported to device drivers for handling.
      3.   Class-C errors occur on device- or port-level public resources. For example, if the physical
           topology of a UB Fabric is damaged, the error can typically be reported to UBFM for
           handling. Class-C errors are either correctable or uncorrectable.
           (1) Correctable errors can fix themselves without affecting services.
           (2) Uncorrectable errors cannot fix themselves and require software intervention.
                 Uncorrectable errors fall into two types: non-fatal uncorrectable error (NFUE) and fatal
                 uncorrectable error (FUE), with FUEs being more severe. Certain uncorrectable errors,
                 as detailed in Appendix D, can be set to NFUEs or FUEs through register configuration.

Devices SHALL be able to detect errors layer by layer from the bottom up and match the detected
errors to the classes defined in this specification.




unifiedbus.com                                                                                            355
10 Resource Management

10.6.2.2.4 List of Class-C Errors

Class-C errors fall into two categories: port-level and device-level, depending on their scope of impact.
If an error cannot be traced to a specific port, it is reported as a device-level error, with the EL field in
the error message being set to 1; otherwise, it is reported as a port-level error, with the EL field in the
error message being set to 0.

Class-C errors typically occur at the physical, data link, and network layers, and those at the physical
and data link layers are all port-level errors. Such errors are recorded in either the Uncorrectable Error
Status or Correctable Error Status register.
