D.5.3 Port Info
                                          Table D-100 Port Info register
                 Bit                                                            Start Offset Default
Field Name                   Description                          Attribute
                 Location                                                       Address      Value
RSVD             31:18       Reserved                             RO_DE0_EO     0x9           NA
Enumeration      17          0: The port is not an                HwInit_DE0_EO 0x9           HwInit
Boundary                     enumeration boundary.
                             1: The port is an enumeration
                             boundary.
Port type        16          Port type.                           RO_DE0_EO     0x9           -
                             0b: physical port
                             1b: vPort
                             vPorts have only the following




unifiedbus.com                                                                                     445
Appendix D Configuration Space Registers




                     Bit                                                                     Start Offset Default
 Field Name                       Description                              Attribute
                     Location                                                                Address      Value
                                  registers: Port Index,
                                  Enumeration Boundary, Port
                                  CNA, and Neighbor Port Info.
 Port Index          15:0         Port Index.                              RO_DE0_EO         0x9                -


Note: Only UB Switches and bus controllers have the Enumeration Boundary register, while other UB Controllers use
natural enumeration boundaries. Both UBFM instances and system software are management roles that initiate
enumeration, yet with different scopes.

        1.   Enumeration by system software: If Enumeration Boundary of a bus controller port is 1, enumeration on that
             path ends; if Enumeration Boundary is 0, which indicates that the boundary has not been reached,
             enumeration continues to the next level to discover private devices.

        2.   Enumeration by a UBFM instance: If Enumeration Boundary of a UB Switch port is 0 and its link neighbor is
             a bus controller, enumeration continues until the bus controller is enumerated even though Enumeration
             Boundary of the bus controller port is 1. This is because UBFM instances are not restricted by the boundary
             port attribute of bus controllers.



D.5.4 Neighbor Port Info
                                         Table D-101 Neighbor Port Info register
                              Bit                                                        Start Offset       Default

D.5.5 Port CNA
Each port of a UB Controller has a CNA. UB Switches do not have this register.

                                              Table D-102 Port CNA register
 Field        Bit                                                                         Start Offset      Default
                              Description                                  Attribute
 Name         Location                                                                    Address           Value
 RSVD         31:24           Reserved                                     RO_DE0_EO      0xF               -
 Port         23:0            The default value is all 0s. Any             RW_DE0_EO      0xF               24'b0
 CNA                          other value means the CNA has
                              been assigned.




unifiedbus.com                                                                                                        446

                                           Table D-103 Port Rst register
 Field           Bit                                                   Start Offset           Default
                                Description        Attribute
 Name            Location                                              Address                Value
 RSVD            31:1           Reserved           RO_DE0_EO           0x10                   NA
 Port Reset      0              Port reset         RW1C_DE0_EO         0x10                   NA
                                control.
                                1: reset
                                0: normal state

All ports have the same PORT_CAP register offsets and meanings but differ in access base addresses.

                            6'h0: reserved
                            6'h1: x1
                            6'h2: x2
                            6'h4: x4

 Current Precode State    3:2                   Precode status of the current data rate.   RO_DE0_EO
                                                For each bit, value 1'b1 indicates that
                                                precode is enabled.
                                                Bit 0: TX precode
                                                Bit 1: RX precode
 Current FEC Mode         7:4                   FEC mode of the current data rate.         RO_DE0_EO
                                                4'h0: FEC bypass
                                                4'h1: RS (128, 120, T=2)
                                                4'h2: RS (128, 120, T=4)
                                                Others: reserved
 Current EQ Mode          11:8                  Current EQ mode.                           RO_DE0_EO
                                                2'h0: Full_EQ
                                                2'h1: Only_Highest_Data_Rate_EQ
                                                2'h2: Skip_EQ
                                                Others: reserved
 Current BCRC Mode        15:12                 BCRC mode of the current data rate.        RO_DE0_EO
                                                4'h0: no BCRC
                                                4'h1: CRC30
                                                Others: reserved

D.6.3.2 Flit Error Log 1

Start Offset Address: 0x1

                                     Table D-142 Flit Error Log 1 register
 Field        Bit
                            Register Description                                               Attribute
 Name         Location
 Flit Error   0             Indicates whether the flit error log is valid.                     RO_DE0_EO
 Log Valid                  When the error is recorded in this register, the value of
                            this bit is 1.
                            Writing 1 to this bit clears this bit, or if more Flit Error Log
                            register entries are valid (for example, bit 13) and set, the
                            Flit Error Log 1 and Flit Error Log 2 registers are loaded
                            as the next oldest log entry.
                            The default value is 0.
 Reserved     31:1          Reserved                                                           RO_DE0_EO

 Flit Error          0               Indicates whether to enable the flit error counter.        RW_DE0_EO

                                     the link.

 Field Name          Bit Location   Register Description                          Attribute
 Equalization        0              Value 1 indicates that the TX equalization    ROS_DE0_EO
 Complete                           process has completed for this rate.

 Equalization        1              Value 1 indicates that the coarsetune         ROS_DE0_EO
 Coarsetune                         phase of the TX equalization process has
 phase Complete                     completed for this rate.
                                    The default value is 0.
 Equalization        2              Value 1 indicates that the coarsetune         ROS_DE0_EO
 Coarsetune                         phase of the TX equalization process has
 Phase Successful                   been successful for this rate.
                                    The default value is 0.
 Equalization        3              Value 1 indicates that the passive phase of   ROS_DE0_EO
 Passive Phase                      the TX equalization process has been
 Successful                         successful for this rate.
                                    The default value is 0.
 Equalization        4              Value 1 indicates that the active phase of    ROS_DE0_EO
 Active Phase                       the TX equalization process has been
 Successful                         successful for this rate.
                                    The default value is 0.
 Link Equalization   5              Hardware sets this bit to 1 to request        RW1CS_DE0_EO
 Request                            execution of the link equalization process
                                    for data rate 1.
                                    The default value is 0.

Appendix D Configuration Space Registers



                              Table D-208 EYE MONITOR Control 1 register
 Field Name                 Bit Location     Register Description                           Attribute
 Eye Monitor Test           2:0              Eye pattern test execution status.             RO_DE0_EO
 Execution Status                            Software can read this register to check the
                                             execution status of the current eye pattern
                                             test. The definition is as follows:
                                             3'b000: idle
                                             3'b001: abort
                                             3'b010: in progress
                                             3'b011: timeout
                                             3'b100: successful
 Reserved                   15:3             Reserved                                       RO_DE0_EO
 Eye Monitor Test           31:16            Eye pattern test result.                       RO_DE0_EO
 Result

D.6.22 PORT_CAP21_PORT_ERR_RECORD

D.6.22.1 Slice Header
                                  Table D-218 PORT_ERR_RECORD slice header
 Field Name                                                                          Value
 Slice Version                                                                       0x0
 Slice Used Size                                                                     0x1E


In the event of a port error, the error status is recorded in the PORT_ERR_RECORD register. Each port
has such a register group. In this register group, registers before 0x10 are public, while registers from
0x10 to 0x1F record port errors. The overall structure is as follows:

Note 1: The unit of the addresses is 4 bytes, which is the same as that of the configuration space.

Note 2: The error status registers of the PORT ERR RECORD and DEVICE ERR RECORD are consistent. However, not
all errors are recorded in PORT ERR RECORD. For details on which register group records specific errors, see the
description of class C errors in Section 10.6.2.2.4.

                              Table D-225 PORT_CAP240_VD slice header

PORT_CAP240_XX to PORT_CAP255_XX are reserved for vendor-defined CAPs.


D.7 CFG0_ROUTE_TABLE

D.7.1 General Requirements
Each routing table entry occupies Y × 4 bytes. Y = [(X – 1) >> 5 + 1], where X is the value of the
CFG0_BASIC.Total Number of Ports register. Routing table entry Z describes the ports for sending
packets to destination network address Z. 0 ≤ Z ≤ N, where N is the value of the Max Index of Route
Table Entry register.




                        Figure D-9 Overall structure of CFG0_ROUTE_TABLE



unifiedbus.com                                                                                        524
Appendix D Configuration Space Registers



                               Table D-226 CFG0 ROUTE TABLE slice header
 Field Name                                            Value
 Slice Version                                         0x0
 Slice Used Size                                       0x10 + (N+1+1)*Y


D.7.2 ROUTE_TABLE_CAP
For routing table entries beyond the declared number range, their register attribute changes to RO.

                                     Table D-227 ROUTE_TABLE_CAP

                   Location                                                     Address        Value

 Entry                            Note: The number of entries is
                                  N+1, where N is the value of
                                  this register.

 Exact Route       16             Indicates whether exact           RO_DE0_EO   0x1            0x0
 Supported                        routes are supported.
                                  0: not supported
                                  1: supported

unifiedbus.com                                                                                                      526
Appendix E Ethernet Interworking




Appendix E Ethernet Interworking


UB networks MAY interwork with Ethernet networks in either of the following ways when using IP
addresses:

      1.   UBPU is directly connected to an Ethernet network through UBoE.
      2.   A UB2E switch is used for conversion between UB links and Ethernet links.
           (1) When a UB2E switch converts the UB link layer to the Ethernet link layer, the information
               of the IP network layer and above SHALL be retained. The NPI MAY be converted to the
               VLAN ID.
           (2) When a UB2E switch converts the Ethernet link layer to the UB link layer, UB network
               extension headers SHALL be added. For details, see Section 5.2.3.
           (3) The UB2E switches MAY support FECN-to-ECN congestion marking conversion, enabling
               unified congestion control across the UB domain and Ethernet domain.




                                   Figure E-1 Ethernet interworking


The preceding two methods both use UBoE packets over the Ethernet. A UBoE packet refers to the
packet format where the UB transaction layer and transport layer are encapsulated over Ethernet.




                                   Figure E-2 UBoE packet format


The jumbo frame function SHALL be enabled on an Ethernet device to prevent packet loss due to the
packet length exceeding the maximum value.




unifiedbus.com                                                                                      527
Appendix F Network Management Based on UB Links




Appendix F Network Management Based on
UB Links


F.1 Applicable Scenarios




                      Figure F-1 UB network based on a traditional server cluster

In traditional server cluster networking scenarios, servers are connected to the switch network through
UB network interface cards (NICs). To ensure compatibility with mainstream management practices for
Ethernet or IP networks, network management protocols over UB links MAY be used for management.

The network ports of UB NICs use UB links, which may directly interconnect with the IP network layer
(hardware type No. 38, UnifiedBus, IANA Address Resolution Protocol Parameters). UB NICs provide
network interface devices for the software stack. Network interface devices use GUIDs as hardware
addresses.


F.2 Management Protocols

F.2.1 Management Packet Format Based on UB Links
         Byte 0                     Byte 1                    Byte 2                     Byte 3
 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0
                                                  LPH
                      Protocol                                         DGUID [127:112]
                                                   ...
                   DGUID [15:0]                                        SGUID [127:112]
                                                   ...
                   SGUID [15:0]                                           Payload
                                                Payload
                      Figure F-2 Management packet format based on UB links




unifiedbus.com                                                                                       528
Appendix F Network Management Based on UB Links



                           Table F-1 Fields of the management packet header
 Field      Bit         Description
 Protocol   16          It identifies the type of the management protocol.
 DGUID      128         It indicates the destination GUID. It is either the GUID of the destination
                        network device or a well-known GUID, used to identify the network device that
                        receives the packet.
 SGUID      128         It indicates the source GUID. It is the GUID of the source network device and
                        is used to identify the network device that sends the packet.


For the management protocols carried on UB links, the LPH.CFG field in the packet header of the UB
link layer SHALL be set to 5. The packets of different management protocols are distinguished by the
Protocol field. The protocols that have been supported are as follows:

                                    Table F-2 Management protocols
 Protocol Field                                     Protocol
 0x0100                                             UB DHCPv4
 0x0101                                             UB DHCPv6
 0x0102                                             IP notify
 0x0103                                             RSVD
 0x0104                                             ULAP
 0x0105                                             RSVD
 0x0106                                             NPIC
 0x0107 to 0x0109                                   RSVD
 0x0110 to 0x0118                                   Customized


F.2.2 Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol




                            Figure F-3 Dynamic IP address management


UB DHCPv4 or DHCPv6 is used to dynamically manage network addresses, involving three
components: the UB DHCP client, UB DHCP relay (optional), and the DHCP server. As shown in the
preceding figure, the servers are connected to the switch through a UB NIC, with the UB DHCP clients
deployed on the servers and the UB DHCP relay on the access switch. When a host requests an IP
address dynamically, it sends the request through a UB DHCP client. The UB DHCP relay assists in the
negotiation between the client and the DHCP server. The UB DHCP relay and DHCP server are
connected through a UB network or an Ethernet network.



unifiedbus.com                                                                                      529
Appendix F Network Management Based on UB Links




Based on DHCP (IETF RFC2131, IETF RFC8415), UB DHCP carries IP packets over the UB links and
specifies the usage rules for certain fields within the DHCP PDU.

The messages exchanged between the UB DHCP client and the directly connected relay or server are
in the following format:

             LPH       Protocol   <SGUID, DGUID>        IP Header     UDP Header      DHCP PDU
                                  Figure F-4 UB DHCP encapsulation format


The field information is as follows:

      1.   The value of LPH.CFG is 5.
      2.   If the value of the Protocol field is 0x0100, it identifies DHCPv4. If the value of the Protocol
           field is 0x0101, it identifies DHCPv6.
      3.   SGUID indicates the GUID of the network device that sends the DHCP packet. Its length is
           128 bits.
      4.   DGUID indicates the GUID of the destination network device or a well-known GUID. Its
           length is 128 bits. The value of low-order 16 bits of a well-known GUID is 0x0100 (DHCPv4)
           or 0x0101 (DHCPv6).

The DHCP PDU field complies with the following rules:

      1.   htype: It SHALL be 38 (UB hardware type).
      2.   hlen: It SHALL be 16.
      3.   chaddr: It SHALL be the GUID of the network device for applying an IP address.
      4.   client-identifier option: It is optional and SHALL be the GUID of the network device.


F.2.3 IP Address Notification Protocol
The IP address notification protocol (IP notify) is triggered when the IP address of a network device is
configured, notifying this IP address to its directly connected neighbor nodes. After parsing the IP notify
that is received, the neighbor nodes can locally generate or update a host route directed to that IP
address, subsequently advertising this routing information across the entire network.




                                               Figure F-5 IP notify


The encapsulation format of IP notify is as follows:

                            LPH     Protocol    <SGUID, DGUID>        IP notify PDU
                                  Figure F-6 IP notify encapsulation format



unifiedbus.com                                                                                            530
Appendix F Network Management Based on UB Links




    1.    The value of LPH.CFG is 5.
    2.    The value of Protocol is 0x0102, identifying IP notify.
    3.    The length of a DGUID is 128 bits. For a well-known GUID, the value of low-order 16 bits of the
          GUID is 0x0102.

The PDU format of IP notify is as follows:

          Byte 0                        Byte 1                    Byte 2                    Byte 3
 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0
  Version          RSVD                  Mask                               IP Address
                                              IP Address (variable)
                                       Figure F-7 PDU format of IP notify


    1.    Version: It indicates the IP address type, with a length of 4 bits. 1 indicates an IPv4 address, 2
          indicates an IPv6 address, and others indicate Reserved.
    2.    Mask: It indicates the number of bits in the subnet mask. Its length is 8 bits.
    3.    IP Address: It indicates the IP address configured for the network device that sends the packet,
          with the length corresponding to Version. The length of an IPv4 address is 4 bytes, and that of
          an IPv6 address is 16 bytes.


F.2.4 UB Link Aggregation Protocol
UB Link Aggregation Protocol (ULAP) is a protocol for dynamic aggregation and disaggregation of UB
links, enabling network devices to automatically form aggregated links based on their configurations
and use these links for sending and receiving data. Building upon the LACP protocol (IEEE 802.1AX),
ULAP encapsulates LACP PDUs over UB links, with the packet format detailed below:

                            LPH        Protocol   <SGUID, DGUID>      LACP PDU
                                  Figure F-8 ULAP encapsulation format


The field information is as follows:

    1.    The value of LPH.CFG is 5.
    2.    The value of Protocol is 0x0104, identifying ULAP.
    3.    The length of a DGUID is 128 bits. For a well-known GUID, the value of low-order 16 bits of the
          GUID is 0x0104.

After the server successfully negotiates with switch 1 and switch 2, the server assigns the same IP
address (bond_IP) to multiple network ports of the bond (mode = 4) and notifies switch 1 and switch 2
through an IP notify message. Switch 1 and switch 2 generate the corresponding bond route and




unifiedbus.com                                                                                          531
Appendix F Network Management Based on UB Links




advertise the route to switch 3. When the bond link is faulty, the bond routes of the switches are
updated in the same way.




                                 Figure F-9 ULAP working mechanism


F.2.5 NPI Configuration Protocol
As an identifier of a network partition, NPI requires trustworthiness of the configuration process. A
trusted configuration SHALL be ensured. If the server software cannot guarantee a trusted
configuration, the NPIs of the UB NICs can be configured on the network. The NPI Configuration
Protocol (NPIC) supports in-band configuration of NPIs and delivers configurations through directly
connected switches.




                                 Figure F-10 NPI configuration process


As shown in the preceding figure, the management system assigns an NPI to each UB NIC.

Step 1: The management system delivers the NPI configuration to the corresponding switch through a
trusted in-band or out-of-band channel. The NPI configuration information may include the NPI
configuration information of the switch ports.

Step 2: The switch sends the received NPI configuration of the UB NIC through the NPIC to the
corresponding UB NIC. The configuration is executed by the UB NIC without involving any server
software.

The NPIC encapsulation format is as follows:




unifiedbus.com                                                                                          532
Appendix F Network Management Based on UB Links




                              LPH      Protocol   <SGUID, DGUID>       NPIC PDU
                                  Figure F-11 NPIC encapsulation format


The field information is as follows:

    1.     The value of LPH.CFG is 5.
    2.     The value of Protocol is 0x0106, identifying the NPIC.
    3.     The length of a DGUID is 128 bits. For a well-known GUID, the value of low-order 16 bits of the
           GUID is 0x0106.

The PDU format of the NPIC is as follows:

           Byte 0                       Byte 1                    Byte 2                      Byte 3
 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0
           Opcode                       RSVD                                     Sum
                                                    GUID 0
                                                    GUID 1
                                                    GUID 2
                                                    GUID 3
                                                      NPI
                                                       ...
                                  Figure F-12 NPIC encapsulation format

      1.    Opcode: Its length is 8 bits. It indicates the operation type. If the value is 1, the configuration
            is delivered. If the value is 2, the configuration is cleared (NPI = 0). For other values, the
            configuration is reserved.
      2.    Sum: Its length is 16 bits. It indicates the number of <GUID, NPI> pairs in the packet.
      3.    GUID: Its length is 128 bits. It indicates the UB NIC.
      4.    NPI: Its length is 32 bits. It indicates the NPI value, with the lower-order 25 bits being valid.


F.2.6 User-Defined Protocol
                                 LPH     Protocol   <SGUID, DGUID>         PDU
                              Figure F-13 User-defined encapsulation format

The network management protocol reserves a protocol range for user-defined network management
protocols. The field information is as follows:

    1.     The value of LPH.CFG is 5.
    2.     The user-defined value of Protocol ranges from 0x109 to 0x118.




unifiedbus.com                                                                                               533
Appendix G Device Hot-Plug




Appendix G Device Hot-Plug


G.1 General Requirements
In a server, UB devices MAY support hot-add and hot-removal. Hot-add allows for dynamic addition of
devices to a running system. Hot-removal enables dynamic removal of devices that are no longer used
or have encountered errors from a running system.




                   Figure G-1 Components enabling device hot-plug in a UB system


To support hot-plug, a server SHALL provide hardware and software components as illustrated in the
figure above, including the user interface, hot-plug service, hot-plug driver, device drivers, slots,
buttons, and indicators. For hot-plug bus controllers, a hot-plug function register group SHALL be
implemented in their CFG0 to support slot description, status display, and hot-plug control. When the
port link status of a UB device changes in a slot, an event SHALL be reported to the hot-plug controller.


G.2 Components Enabling Device Hot-Plug
The following table lists the hardware and software components that SHALL be implemented to enable
device hot-plug.

                              Table G-1 Components enabling device hot-plug
 Component               Function Description

 Indicator               Indicates the slot status. For details, see the following description about
                         indicators.
 Button                  Notifies the system that a device needs to be removed.
 Power management        Controlled by system software to power on or off slots.
 module




unifiedbus.com                                                                                          534
Appendix G Device Hot-Plug




 Component                Function Description

 Slot                     Serves as the basic component to provide the hot-plug capability.
 Power controller         Controls slot power supply through software and detects whether the slot
                          power status is normal.
 User interface           Displays device status and other information for users based on system
                          software that acquires the slot status through hardware and software
                          interfaces.


        1.   Indicators
             Each slot has two indicators: a device working status indicator and a power status indicator.
             Each indicator has three states: on, off, and blinking.

                                   Table G-2 Device working status indicator
 Indicator State           Description

 Off                       The device is operating normally, and device removal is not allowed.
 On                        The device is not in use or is faulty, and if necessary, needs to be manually
                           troubleshooted.
 Blinking                  The device is currently being requested for operations by a user and can be
                           safely removed.


                                       Table G-3 Power status indicator
 Indicator State           Description

 Off                       The slot is powered off, and the device can be added or removed.
 On                        The slot is powered on, and the device cannot be added or removed.
 Blinking                  The slot is currently being requested for operations by a user, and the device
                           cannot be added or removed.


        2.   Buttons
             A button SHALL be configured for each slot for notification of hot-removal or hot-add.
             Pressing a button will trigger system software to determine the presence status of the device
             in the slot, and then: (1) hot-removal is performed, if the device is currently present and
             operating; or (2) hot-add is performed, if the device is not present or the slot is powered off.
             The implementation of buttons is not described in this specification.
        3.   Power management module
             The power management module controls slot power-on and power-off based on the control
             components on server boards and system software. It is RECOMMENDED that the module
             power off a slot before the device in the slot is removed through hot-plug operations. When
             there is no device in the slot, system software may power off the slot, so that it ceases to
             function. If a device needs to be added to a slot through hot-plug operations, it is




unifiedbus.com                                                                                              535
Appendix G Device Hot-Plug




           RECOMMENDED that the device be connected to the slot while the slot is powered off, and
           then system software powers on the slot and initializes the connected device.
      4.   Slots
           Hot-plug is performed on a slot-by-slot basis. A single slot can contain one or more
           consecutively numbered ports. When a slot contains multiple ports, hot-plug must be
           performed simultaneously across all these ports. The number of slots supported by a device
           and the maximum number of ports contained within each slot depend on the physical
           implementation of the device. The CFG0_CAP2_SHP register of Entity 0 specifies the
           number of supported slots and the slot-specific port information.
      5.   Power controller
           The power controller is an optional component for device hot-plug. It provides an interface for
           software to control the slot power status. The power controller SHALL be able to detect the
           slot power status.
      6.   User interface
           The user interface helps users handle hot-plug operations. In systems that support multiple
           hot-plug slots, the user interface allows users to initialize each slot independently.


G.3 Hot-Removal Process
The process of removing a device through hot-plug operations is as follows:

      1.   Notifying system software of hot-removal
           A user presses a button on a slot to notify system software that the device in the slot needs
           to be removed. When the button is pressed, a button message of the slot SHALL be sent to
           the hot-plug controller to inform the controller of an impending hot-plug event. The hot-plug
           controller can obtain the slot number from Slot Index in the button message.
      2.   Checking the device status
           The device driver SHALL ensure that the device in the slot has no tasks to execute by
           checking the device status, and it is RECOMMENDED that system software wait until the
           device is idle before proceeding with hot-removal. As a slot supports multiple port
           connections, the device driver needs to verify that all devices connected to ports have no
           unfinished tasks before considering the devices as idle.
      3.   Determining that the device can be safely removed
           After determining that the device is idle, system software controls the device working status
           indicator to show that the device is not in use. Then the device driver can be uninstalled.
           The presence status of the port device changes, triggering a device presence interrupt.
      4.   Clearing the device information and removing the device
           System software processes the device presence interrupt. It determines that the device is
           not present, reclaims device resources, and then clears the routing information
           corresponding to the port.




unifiedbus.com                                                                                           536
Appendix G Device Hot-Plug




           After reclaiming the device resources, system software may power off the slot. Then, the
           device can be safely removed.


G.4 Hot-Add Process
The process of adding a device through hot-plug operations is as follows:

      1.   Adding a device to a powered-off slot
           A user checks the slot power status indicator to confirm that the slot is powered off and then
           adds a device to the slot.
      2.   Pressing the button to trigger slot power-on
           When the slot is in the power-off state, the user presses the button to trigger slot power-on.
           Then, system software modifies the device presence status, triggering a device presence
           interrupt.
      3.   Checking the device presence status
           System software, upon receiving the device presence interrupt, checks the status of the
           corresponding slot to confirm that the device is present. Then, the device status changes to
           hot-add.
      4.   Adding the device
           System software processes the device presence interrupt, performs device scanning for the
           newly powered-on slot, adds the device, allocates resources to the device, and then
           distributes routing information.
      5.   Loading the device driver
           System software loads the device driver and initializes the device. Then, the device can be
           used normally.


G.5 Hot-Plug Events
Hot-plug slots SHALL be able to handle the following events.

      1.   Button operation event
           When receiving a button operation event, the hot-plug controller SHALL report a hot-plug
           event to the specified destination by sending the control register indication according to the
           hot-plug message.
      2.   Device presence detection event
           When receiving an event indicating that the device presence status has changed, the hot-
           plug controller SHALL report a hot-plug event to the specified destination by sending the
           control register indication according to the hot-plug message.




unifiedbus.com                                                                                         537
Appendix G Device Hot-Plug




      3.   Port link status detection event
           When receiving an event indicating that the link status has changed, the hot-plug controller
           SHALL report a hot-plug event to the specified destination by sending the control register
           indication according to the hot-plug message.

A hot-plug event may be lost during transmission, leading to a reporting failure. Therefore, users
SHALL confirm the receipt of the event before adding or removing a device; otherwise, hot-plug
needs to be re-triggered.

For a device that is currently undergoing hot-plug, the hot-plug event may arrive at the specified
destination before or after other messages. Therefore, when processing other messages, the destination
SHALL be able to perform fault tolerance handling. If the destination receives other messages from a
device that has already been removed through hot-plug operations, it SHALL discard them.




unifiedbus.com                                                                                          538
Appendix H URPC Message Format




Appendix H URPC Message Format


H.1 Overview
This chapter lists the message formats supported by URPC. For detailed URPC design specifications,
refer to Section 8.5 (URPC).


H.2 URPC Function


                                     Figure H-1 URPC function fields


URPC uses function fields to identify the specific function to be called on a UBPU. URPC functions are
categorized into two types: (i) fixed public functions (i.e. public methods) and (ii) customized functions
(i.e. customized methods).

Public methods maintain a consistent definition—including functions, APIs, and parameters—within the
same UBPU class or subclass. Customized methods include user-defined functions and APIs tailored to
specific scenarios, on which the URPC protocol imposes no constraints. These customized methods
can be dynamically deployed or undeployed on the server/worker. When deployed, they become
available for client calls.

                                         Table H-1 URPC function fields
 Field Name            Bit Width (bit)       Description
 UBPU class            12                    Indicates the type of the UBPU.
 UBPU subclass         12                    Indicates the specific subtype of the UBPU.
                                             Indicates the nature of the method.
 P[rivate]             1                     0: Fixed public function (public method)
                                             1: Customized function (customized method)
 Method                23                    Indicates the URPC function.


The client can use predefined methods to query public/customized functions and deploy or undeploy
customized functions on the server/worker.

The URPC protocol reserves the following function IDs and their associated P[rivate] and method field
values. These IDs have specific, predefined meanings and must not be used for user-customized
functions.




unifiedbus.com                                                                                          539
Appendix H URPC Message Format




      ⚫    Public methods:
           −   0b0000_0000_0000_0000_0000_0000: Queries the supported public functions of the
               UBPU.
      ⚫    Customized methods:
           −   0b1000_0000_0000_0000_0000_0000: Queries the supported customized functions of
               the UBPU.
           −   0b1000_0000_0000_0000_0000_0001: Deploys new customized function.
           −   0b1000_0000_0000_0000_0000_0010: Undeploys the customized function.
           −   0b1000_0000_0000_0000_0000_0011: Queries the method identified with a specified
               name.

                           Table H-2 URPC public function fields in storage domain
 UBPU Class                  UBPU Subclass             P                         Method
 [47:36]                     [35:24]                   [23:23]                   [22:0]
 0x002                       0x001                     0                         …


H.3 URPC Messages
URPC messages constitute the interaction mechanism between the URPC client and server. Their
formats are defined as follows:


H.3.1 Field Definition
                                       Table H-3 URPC field definition
 Field Name         Bit Width (bit)     Description
 Type               4                   Indicates the URPC message format.
                                        0: URPC request.
                                        1: URPC acknowledgment.
                                        2: URPC response.
                                        3: URPC acknowledgment and response (The message format
                                        is identical to the URPC response).




unifiedbus.com                                                                                   540
Appendix H URPC Message Format




H.3.2 URPC Request




                                 Figure H-2 URPC request fields


                                   Table H-4 URPC request fields
 Field Name           Bit Width (bit)   Description
 Version              4                 The URPC version number. The current version number is 1.
 Type                 4                 Indicates the URPC message format.
                                        0: URPC request.
 Acknowledgment       1                 Indicates whether a URPC acknowledgment is required
                                        during the URPC call process. The server decides
                                        autonomously whether to merge the URPC acknowledgment
                                        with the URPC response.
                                        0: A URPC acknowledgment is required.
                                        1: A URPC acknowledgment is not required.
 Argument DMA         6                 The number of Argument DMA operations the server needs
 count                                  to perform.
                                        When the argument DMA count equals 0, the argument DMA
                                        table does not exist.
 Function             48                Indicates the specific function called by the URPC.
 Request total size   32                Indicates the total size of the URPC request, in bytes. This
                                        size includes the protocol header and all data segments but
                                        excludes the size of the argument DMA table.
 Request ID           32                A unique identifier for the URPC call, used to match requests
                                        with corresponding acknowledgments/responses.
 Client's URPC        24                Indicates the client-side URPC channel used for sending
 channel                                URPC requests and receiving URPC
                                        acknowledgments/responses.
 Function defined     8                 Indicates the specific domain the function targets, which
                                        determines the type of the URPC extension header used.
                                        0: No extension header
                                        1: General-purpose computing extension header




unifiedbus.com                                                                                      541
Appendix H URPC Message Format




 Field Name          Bit Width (bit)    Description
                                        2: Storage PLOG extension header
                                        Others: To be defined
 Argument DMA size   32                 The size of the argument DMA performed by the server.
 Argument DMA's      64                 The UB address of the argument DMA performed by the
 UB address                             server.
 Argument DMA's      32                 The UB token associated with the argument DMA performed
 UB token                               by the server.
 Extension header    Customizable       The extension protocol for the URPC request, which can be
                                        customized based on the specific domain.
 User data           Customizable       User data directly transferred in the URPC request.
 Inline data         Customizable       The data directly transferred in the URPC request, including
                                        the URPC extension header and user data.


H.3.3 URPC Acknowledgment




                           Figure H-3 URPC acknowledgment fields


                             Table H-5 URPC acknowledgment fields
 Field Name           Bit Width (bit)     Description
 Version              4                   The URPC version number. The current version number is
                                          1.
 Type                 4                   Indicates the URPC message format.
                                          1: Only the URPC acknowledgment.
                                          3: Both the URPC acknowledgment and response. The
                                          message format is the same as that of the URPC
                                          response.
 Request ID range     16                  Indicates the range of request IDs included in the URPC
                                          acknowledgments/responses. The collective transmission
                                          of URPC acknowledgments/responses must meet the
                                          following conditions:
                                          ● Consecutive request IDs
                                          ● Shared URPC channel
                                          ● Shared status
                                          By default, the value of the request ID range is 1, meaning
                                          it only includes the URPC response corresponding to the
                                          current request ID.
 Request ID           32                  A unique identifier for the URPC call, used to match




unifiedbus.com                                                                                     542
Appendix H URPC Message Format




 Field Name            Bit Width (bit)      Description
                                            requests with corresponding acknowledgments/responses.
 Client's URPC         24                   Indicates the client-side URPC channel used for sending
 channel                                    URPC requests and receiving URPC
                                            acknowledgments/responses.


H.3.4 URPC Response




                                   Figure H-4 URPC response fields


                                    Table H-6 URPC response fields
 Field Name      Bit Width (bit)      Description
 Version         4                    The URPC version number. The current version number is 1.
 Type            4                    Indicates the URPC message format.
                                      2: Only the URPC response.
                                      3: Both the URPC acknowledgment and response. The
                                      message format is the same as that of the URPC response.
 Status          8                    0: The worker successfully executed the URPC.
                                      1: The server rejects the execution request.
                                      2: Function calling is not supported.
                                      3: The server has insufficient argument buffer.
                                      4: URPC call timed out.
                                      5: Version unmatched.
                                      6: URPC protocol header error.
                                      Others: To be defined
 Request ID      16                   Indicates the range of request IDs included in the URPC
 range                                acknowledgments/responses. The collective transmission of
                                      URPC acknowledgments/responses must meet the following
                                      conditions:
                                      ● Consecutive request IDs
                                      ● Shared URPC channel
                                      ● Shared status
                                      By default, the value of the request ID range is 1, meaning it



unifiedbus.com                                                                                         543
Appendix H URPC Message Format




 Field Name        Bit Width (bit)   Description
                                     only includes the URPC response corresponding to the current
                                     request ID.
 Request ID        32                A unique identifier for the URPC call, used to match requests
                                     with corresponding acknowledgments/responses.
 Client's URPC     24                Indicates the client-side URPC channel used for sending URPC
 channel                             requests and receiving URPC acknowledgments/responses.
 Function          8                 Indicates the specific domain the function targets, which
 defined                             determines the type of the URPC extension header used.
                                     0: No extension header
                                     1: General-purpose computing extension header
                                     2: Storage PLOG extension header
                                     Others: To be defined
 Response total    32                Indicates the total size of the URPC response, in bytes. This
 size                                size includes the protocol header and return data but excludes
                                     the size of return data offsets.
 Return data       32                When return data size exists, this field indicates the return data
 offset                              offset for each request ID within the collectively transferred
                                     response data for an URPC response.
                                     The return data offset for the first URPC request in the collective
                                     transfer, calculated as "request ID – request ID range + 1",
                                     defaults to 0 and is therefore omitted. Consequently, the
                                     sequence of return data offset values only applies to subsequent
                                     request IDs, starting from "request ID – request ID range + 2" up
                                     to the current request ID.
                                     The return data offset does not exist if:
                                     ● The return data itself does not exist.
                                     ● The request ID range equals 1.

 External header   Customizable      The extension protocol for the URPC response, which can be
                                     customized based on the specific domain.
 User data         Customizable      User data directly transferred in the URPC request.
 Return data       Customizable      Return data transferred in the URPC response, including the
                                     URPC extension header and user data.




unifiedbus.com                                                                                        544
Appendix I Application Example




Appendix I Application Example


I.1 Storage PLOG

I.1.1 Overview
The URPC protocol is used in storage systems to enable the unified calling of storage device functions.
Its key features include:

      ⚫    Pass-by-reference support: Allows storage data to be passed as arguments by reference.
      ⚫    Unified function call behavior: Provides consistent interfaces for both CPU-mediated and
           direct access to storage devices. This allows programmers to focus solely on service
           implementation, regardless of the URPC server's form.




                            Figure I-1 URPC PLOG storage application scenario


The PLOG, a scale-up storage persistent protocol, encapsulates data before calling URPC interfaces
for storage access. This approach supports two scenarios:

      ⚫    CPU → CPU → SSD: A URPC request initiated by the CPU is first processed by the target
           CPU and then accesses the solid-state drive (SSD).
      ⚫    CPU → SSU: A URPC request initiated by the CPU directly accesses the UB solid-state
           unit (SSU).




unifiedbus.com                                                                                        545
Appendix I Application Example




I.1.2 URPC PLOG Extension Message
For high-performance storage access, the URPC PLOG extension message, an extension transmission
protocol, is defined within the URPC message's extension header. This protocol is designed to
accommodate the features of storage services.

The extension header is enabled when the function defined equals 2.


I.1.2.1 URPC PLOG Extension Request




                           Figure I-2 URPC PLOG extension request fields


                            Table I-1 URPC PLOG extension request fields
 Field Name            Bit Width (bit)   Description
 URPC request          20 × 8            URPC request base header. For detailed definitions of the
                                         URPC request fields, refer to Appendix H.3.2 (URPC
                                         Request).
 Version               4                 The version number for URPC PLOG extension request.
                                         The current version number is 1.
 Data transfer mode    4                 The method of data transfer.
                                         0: Send (push). The client uses a send operation to push
                                         request data to the server.
                                         1: Send (push/pull). The client uses a send operation to
                                         push request data to the server. If the server encounters
                                         insufficient memory, the server uses read operations to pull
                                         data from the client.
                                         2: Send + read. The client uses a send operation to push
                                         request data to the server. The server uses read operations
                                         to pull data from the client.
                                         3: Send + write. The client uses a send operation to push
                                         the response data address to the server. The server then
                                         uses write operations to return the response to the client,




unifiedbus.com                                                                                         546
Appendix I Application Example




 Field Name            Bit Width (bit)   Description
                                         ensuring the response data is returned before the response
                                         result.
                                         4: Send + write with immediate. The client uses a send
                                         operation to push the response data address to the server.
                                         The server then uses write with immediate operations to
                                         return the response to the client, with the client ensuring the
                                         response data is returned before the response result.
                                         5: Send + read + write. The client uses a send operation to
                                         push both the request and response data addresses to the
                                         server. The server then uses read operations to pull the
                                         request data from the client and uses write operations to
                                         return the response to the client.
 User header offset    8                 The offset of the user header relative to the start of the
                                         extension header.
 User data offset      16                The offset of the user or SGL data relative to the start of the
                                         extension header.
 DMA data segment      16                The number of DMA data segments in the request.
 count                                   ● If the value is 0, only user data is present.
                                         ● If the value is greater than 0, only SGL data is present,
                                           and the field indicates the number of SGEs within the
                                           SGL data.
 Local queue ID        16                The ID of the local queue, unique within the process. It is
                                         used as an index to correlate local queue context
                                         information (e.g. available target Jetties) after receiving a
                                         remote response.
                                         0: Invalid value.
 Server's URPC         24                The channel ID assigned by the server for the client's
 channel                                 connection.
 Early response        1                 Indicates whether the server should return a response.
                                         0: The server needs to return a response.
                                         1: The server does not need to return a response.
 Flow control flag     2                 Indicates whether the message is subject to flow control
 (FC)                                    scheduling.
                                         0: Yes.
                                         1: No.
 DMA redirection       1                 Specifies whether the DMA destination is redirected to a
                                         third-party UBPU (neither the client nor the server). If
                                         redirection is enabled, the specific destination is determined
                                         by the DMA data's UB EID index.
                                         0: Disabled. The default DMA destination is the client-side
                                         UBPU.
                                         1: Enabled.
 Local queue token     32                The token value of the local queue, used to verify queue
 value                                   validity upon response return.




unifiedbus.com                                                                                           547
Appendix I Application Example




 Field Name            Bit Width (bit)   Description
 Security header       28 × 8            Stores initialization vector (12 bytes) and tag (16 bytes)
                                         information when encryption/decryption and integrity
                                         verification are enabled.
 User header           192 × 8           User-defined (PLOG) protocol.
 DMA data's UB         64                The address for DMA data.
 address
 DMA data size         32                The data size for DMA.
 DMA data SGL type     8                 Indicates how the server should handle the associated SGE.
                                         0x0B: The server processes this SGE.
                                         0x1B: The server skips this SGE and continues processing
                                         the next.
 DMA data's UB         20                The token ID for DMA data, used for validity verification of
 token ID                                remote memory access.
 DMA data's UB EID     12                The UB EID index for the DMA target, used to support DMA
 index                                   redirection to a third-party UBPU beyond the client or
                                         server.
 DMA data's UB         32                The UB token value for DMA data, used for validity
 token value                             verification of remote memory access.
 DMA data flag         4                 DMA data attribute flag. It indicates whether the server will
                                         read data from or write data to the client's memory address.
                                         0: Read.
                                         1: Write.
                                         Constraint: Read and write operations cannot be
                                         interleaved. All SGEs for read operations must precede
                                         those for write operations.

                           Figure I-3 URPC PLOG extension response fields

 mode                                 0: Send (push). The client uses a send operation to push request

                                      1: Send (push/pull). The client uses a send operation to push

 channel

unifiedbus.com                                                                                            549
Appendix I Application Example




I.1.3 Data Transfer Modes




                                 Figure I-4 URPC PLOG transfer mode 0


The workflow for data transfer mode 0 is illustrated in the figure. Key steps include:

      1.   The caller initiates a function call, passing the request data.
      2.   The client encapsulates the URPC protocol header and uses a send operation to push the
           header and request data to the server.




                                 Figure I-5 URPC PLOG transfer mode 1




unifiedbus.com                                                                                  550
Appendix I Application Example




The workflow for data transfer mode 1 is illustrated in the figure. Key steps include:

      1.   The caller initiates a function call, passing the request data.
      2.   The client encapsulates the URPC protocol header and uses a send operation to push the
           header data, request data, and request data's address to the server.
      3.   Upon receiving the data and address, the server performs a memory check:
           ▪ If the server has sufficient memory, it directly receives the request data.
           ▪ If the server has insufficient memory, it first requests memory allocation.
             - If the allocation is successful, the server uses read operations to pull the request data
                 from the client based on the provided data address.
             - If the allocation fails, an exception response is returned to the server.




                                 Figure I-6 URPC PLOG transfer mode 2


The workflow for data transfer mode 2 is illustrated in the figure. Key steps include:

      1.   The caller initiates a function call, passing the request data.
      2.   The client encapsulates the URPC protocol header and uses a send operation to push the
           header data and request data's address to the server.
      3.   The server requests memory allocation.
           ▪ If the allocation is successful, the server uses read operations to pull the request data
              from the client based on the provided data address.
           ▪ If the allocation fails, an exception response is returned to the server.




unifiedbus.com                                                                                           551
Appendix I Application Example




                                 Figure I-7 URPC PLOG transfer mode 3


The workflow for data transfer mode 3 is illustrated in the figure. Key steps include:

      1.   The caller initiates a function call, passing the response data address.
      2.   The client encapsulates the URPC protocol header and uses a send operation to push the
           header data and response data's address to the server.
      3.   After the server executes the function, it uses write operations to write the response data to
           the response data's address.
      4.   Upon successfully writing the response data, the server sends the response result to the client.
      5.   After receiving the response result, the client returns the function call result.




unifiedbus.com                                                                                         552
Appendix I Application Example




                                 Figure I-8 URPC PLOG transfer mode 4


The workflow for data transfer mode 4 is illustrated in the figure. Key steps include:

      1.   The caller initiates a function call, passing the response data address.
      2.   The client encapsulates the URPC protocol header and uses a send operation to push the
           header data and response data's address to the server.
      3.   After the server executes the function, it uses write with immediate operations to write the
           response data to the response data's address and sends the response result to the client.
      4.   After confirming the response data and result, the client returns the function call result.




                                 Figure I-9 URPC PLOG transfer mode 5




unifiedbus.com                                                                                            553
Appendix I Application Example




The workflow for data transfer mode 5 is illustrated in the figure. Key steps include:

      1.   The caller initiates a function call, passing both the request data address (for subsequent
           read operations) and the response data address (for subsequent write operations).
      2.   The client encapsulates the URPC protocol header and uses a send operation to push the
           header data, request data's address, and response data's address to the server.
      3.   After the server executes the function, it simultaneously uses read operations to pull the
           request data from the client and employs write operations to write the response data to the
           response data's address.

                                      Table I-3 Data transfer modes
                          Recommended
 Data Transfer Mode                                  Features
                          Scenario
                                                     ● Direct data transfer completes in only half a
 Data transfer mode 0:    Small I/O request
                                                       round trip, ensuring low latency.
 send (push)              transfer                   ● The server needs to pre-allocate sufficient
                                                       memory to receive requests, leading to high
                                                       memory occupation and low utilization.
                                                     ● When server memory is sufficient, direct data
 Data transfer mode 1:    Small I/O request
                                                       transfer completes in only half a round trip,
 send (push/pull)         transfer                     ensuring low latency.
                                                     ● The switching process between push and pull is
                                                       complex, and performance disparities between
                                                       the two can lead to memory access latency
                                                       jitter. Request transfer typically completes
                                                       within 0.5 or 1.5 round trips.
                                                     ● The server does not need to pre-allocate
                                                       extensive memory for receiving requests. When
                                                       server memory is insufficient, the system
                                                       flexibly switches to the pull mode to handle the
                                                       overflow.
                                                     ● 1.5 round trips are required to complete request
 Data transfer mode 2:    Large I/O request
                                                       transfer.
 send + read              transfer                   ● The server does not need to pre-allocate
                                                       extensive memory for receiving requests.
                          ● Large I/O response       ● 1.5 round trips are required to complete
 Data transfer mode 3:
                            transfer                   response transfer.
 send + write (server                                ● The client does not need to pre-allocate
                          ● Short network
 order-preserving)          transmission               extensive memory for receiving responses.
                            distance                 ● Server resources are consumed for order-
                                                       preserving operations.
                          ● Large I/O response       ● Half a round trip is required to complete
 Data transfer mode 4:
                            transfer                   response transfer.
 send + write with                                   ● The client does not need to pre-allocate
                          ● Long network
 immediate (client          transmission               extensive memory for receiving responses.
 order-preserving)          distance                 ● Client resources are consumed for order-
                                                       preserving operations.




unifiedbus.com                                                                                           554
Appendix I Application Example




                         Recommended
 Data Transfer Mode                              Features
                         Scenario
                                                 ● 1.5 round trips are required to complete request
 Data transfer mode 5:   Large I/O request and
                                                   transfer.
 send + read + write     response transfer       ● 1.5 round trips are required to complete
                                                   response transfer.
                                                 ● The server does not need to pre-allocate
                                                   extensive memory for receiving requests.
                                                 ● The client does not need to pre-allocate
                                                   extensive memory for receiving responses.
